activity_metadata.objective: Students will analyze how Deza responds to receiving a C+ on her essay and use their understanding of her character to write a narrative scene imagining her approach to Mr. Smith's next assignment.
activity_metadata.alignment_long: This activity replaces the Write section from the Close Read activity for The Mighty Miss Malone. It offers a scaffolded sequence that guides students through analyzing Deza's response to receiving a C+ and writing a narrative scene imagining her next interaction with Mr. Smith.
activity_metadata.alignment_short: Close Read: Analyzing Deza’s Response and Narrative Writing
activity_metadata.excerpt:
The Mighty Miss Malone

From Chapter Twenty-Two: Learning How to Settle in Flint

(17) In English class I really showed how much I’d toughened up.

(18) Flint teachers don’t have the imaginations that Gary teachers do, so instead of giving grades back so everyone knows what you got, they walk around the class and hand your test or paper back to you. Upside down.

(19) Mr. Smith was passing out our first essay. I’d followed all of Mrs. Needham’s advice. I’d written it at the Flint Public Library and was very careful not to use the dictionary or the thesaurus too much. And I didn’t digress at all.

(20) I made sure my posture was good, crossed my ankles and folded my hands on the desk when he got close to me.

(21) He handed me my paper and smiled. “Very good job.”

(22) My heart flew! “Thank you, sir.”

(23) I turned my paper over. He’d written, “Good for you!” and put a giant C+ with three exclamation points.

(24) I turned the paper back over. Maybe I saw it wrong.

(25) I looked again but it was the same.

(26) One sign that I had toughened up was that instead of crying I thought of a little joke that Jimmie said he did whenever he didn’t like his grade.

(27) “I turn the paper over, then, the same way people bang on a machine if it ain’t acting right, I smack my hand on the paper. Maybe if I bang it hard enough my grade will jump up a mark!”

(28) It was nonsense, but I slapped my hand on Mr. Smith’s essay.

(29) I turned the paper back over and smiled.

(30) I’d have to tell Jimmie that it still wasn’t working.

(31) Mrs. Needham would’ve been proud. Instead of bawling, I looked at Mr. Smith’s back and said to myself, “OK, buster, I’m going to make sure my next essay is the best thing I’ve ever written. You won’t have any choice but to give me my A plus.”

(32) When me and Loretta were walking back to camp I asked, “What grade did you get on your essay?”

(33) “I don’t know, the same old D. What’d you get?”

(34) “C plus.”

(35) She stopped walking.

(36) “Uh-oh, no, you didn’t!”

(37) I showed her my grade.

(38) “Ooh, girl, you must be real smart.”

(39) “For getting a C plus?”

(40) “All these teachers up here at Whittier’s prejudice. Katherine Williams was the smartest colored girl in the school and all she use to get was a C. You must be a genius to get a C plus!”

(41) She laughed. “I'm gonna see if I can sit next to you when we take our next exam!”

questions_map.question: Highlight a detail from paragraphs 20-29 that shows how Deza responds EXTERNALLY (her actions) to receiving the C+.
Next, highlight a line from paragraphs 22-31 that shows how Deza responds INTERNALLY (her thoughts) to receiving the C+.
How do Deza's external and internal responses to the C+ differ from each other?
Highlight a detail from paragraphs 17-23 that reveals something about Mr. Smith's character or teaching style.
Now, highlight a detail from paragraphs 32-41 that shows Loretta's reaction when she learns about Deza's C+ grade.
Based on Loretta's explanation about grades at Whittier, what does Deza's C+ actually suggest about Mr. Smith's opinion of her work?
Based on what you've learned about Deza's character and her response to the C+, what specific actions or strategies might she use when approaching Mr. Smith's next assignment?

Consider how Deza responds to the C+ she received on her essay, both internally and externally. Then imagine how she will approach the next assignment she receives from Mr. Smith. Use the information you learned about both characters, as well as Deza’s other interactions with characters in the text, to write a short scene that describes this event.

USER_14.question: Based on what you've learned about Deza's character and her response to the C+, what specific actions or strategies might she use when approaching Mr. Smith's next assignment?

responses.content:
Based on her character and response to the C+, Deza is likely to approach Mr. Smith's next assignment with increased effort, strategic planning, and a shift in perspective.
Deza's character traits that inform her approach
Highly intelligent and a hard worker: Deza is academically gifted and dedicated to her schoolwork, but her stubborn, confident nature can make her resistant to advice at first.
Determined and resilient: Though initially disappointed and frustrated by the C+, Deza uses the setback as motivation. She is committed to improving and not letting the low grade define her.
Values external approval: As the top student in her class, Deza is competitive and cares about being the smartest. The C+ is a shock to her self-image as the best, which motivates her to prove her worth to Mr. Smith and herself.
Deza's specific actions and strategies
1. Strategic planning and research
Outlining ideas: Deza will likely take Mr. Smith's advice to outline her ideas before writing, rather than jumping straight into the assignment. This extra preparation will help her organize her thoughts and create a more cohesive essay.
Breaking down the task: She will probably break the assignment down into smaller, manageable steps. This will help her manage her time and avoid feeling overwhelmed by the project.
Conducting research: As a curious and inquisitive student, Deza will likely conduct thorough research to better understand the assignment's topic, ensuring her paper is comprehensive and well-supported.
2. Shift in perspective
Infusing personal voice: Recognizing that her writing style may need to mature, she will focus on incorporating her unique voice and insights, rather than just focusing on the mechanics of the essay. This shows she is capable of learning and adapting when given constructive criticism.
Embracing feedback: Instead of shying away from feedback, she will likely be more open to it. Deza might reach out to classmates for input, using their perspectives to help her grow as a writer.
3. Seeking support
Communicating with Mr. Smith: Deza may have a direct conversation with Mr. Smith to better understand his expectations and what he is looking for in a top-tier essay. This will allow her to show her commitment and address his feedback head-on.
Collaborating with classmates: In the past, Deza has benefited from collaborating with her friends on group projects. She will likely tap into this support system, asking for feedback and engaging in a collaborative process rather than tackling the project alone.
By implementing these new actions, Deza will transform her initial disappointment and frustration into a determined strategy for future success (i did use my own words.)
